summary: Drew DeVault argues that pkg.go.dev represents Google's corporate interests overriding good engineering principles in the Go ecosystem. He contends that Go's quality comes from its Bell Labs-influenced designers, not from Google's stewardship, and that pkg.go.dev exemplifies typical Google crapware. The site launched as closed-source, routes module fetches through a closed-source tracking proxy, and hard-codes a list of centralized git hosting services while ignoring the decentralized nature of git. DeVault sees the project as prioritizing a shiny product over sound engineering, adding to evidence that Google is harmful to Go's development.
key_points: Go's quality derives from Bell Labs-influenced language designers, not Google's stewardship — pkg.go.dev reinforces this distinction
pkg.go.dev routes all module fetches through proxy.golang.org, a closed-source proxy that tracks users without their knowledge
The site hard-codes centralized git hosting services, ignoring that git hosting is software, not just a platform — self-hosted instances are unsupported
Go's package discovery requires language-specific meta tags in git hosting HTML, imposing programming language needs onto language-agnostic version control
The project prioritizes visual polish over engineering quality, introducing dozens of regressions while only improving aesthetics
money_quotes: it has all the hallmarks of Google crapware and none of the deliberate, good engineering work that went into Go's design

Go is a pretty good programming language. I have long held that this is not attributable to Google's stewardship, but rather to a small number of language designers and a clear line of influences which is drawn entirely from outside of Google — mostly from Bell Labs.
a closed-source proxy through which all of your go module fetches are being routed and tracked (oh, you didn't know? They never told you, after all)
pkg.go.dev hard-codes a list of centralized git hosting services, and completely disregards the idea of git hosting as software rather than as a platform
The hubris to impose your programming language's needs onto a language-agnostic version control system!
The goal is shipping a shiny new product — not engineering a good solution. This is typical of Google's engineering ethos in general.
pkg.go.dev sucks, and is added the large (and growing) body of evidence that Google is bad for Go
key_insight: Google's control over Go's ecosystem infrastructure prioritizes centralization and corporate convenience over the open, decentralized engineering principles that made Go good in the first place.
